sections.heading: Degrees of certainty
sections.body: Must (95%+): logical conclusion from evidence. Might/may/could (30–60%): possibility, not certain. Can't/couldn't (near 0%): logical impossibility — contradicts evidence.

sections.heading: Past deduction
sections.body: Add 'have + past participle' for deduction about the past: 'She must have forgotten.' 'He might have left already.' 'That can't have been her — she was abroad.'

examples.english: She must be exhausted — she's been awake for 30 hours.
He might be in a meeting.
That can't be right — I checked the figures myself.
She must have missed the train.

exercises.instruction: Use must, might, or can't to react to each situation.
items: The light is on and music is playing. (someone is home)
She's not answering her phone. (busy / asleep / out)
He says he's 7 feet tall. (impossible / unlikely)
She got 100% on every test. (very intelligent)

quiz.question: 'That can't be Tom — he moved to Australia.' 'Can't' here expresses:
quiz.explanation: 'Can't be' expresses logical impossibility based on evidence — equivalent to 'it's impossible that it is Tom'.
